text:
Admission Date:  2007-12-28       Discharge Date:  2018-10-30

Date of Birth:   2005-12-13       Sex:  M

Service:  CCU

ADDENDUM:  This dictation encompasses the date 2007-12-28 to
2018-10-30.

HISTORY OF THE PRESENT ILLNESS:  This is a 58-year-old male
with a history of diabetes, heroin abuse, liver transplant
six years ago, a recent admission for community-acquired
pneumonia to an outside hospital, discharged two days prior
to admission, who presented back to Desert View Regional Medical Center Hospital
with 12 hours of substernal chest pain.  He was found in the
Emergency Department to have an anterior ST elevation MI,
loaded on Plavix, started on Integrelin, heparin, IV
Lopressor, and sent to Baptist Medical Center for angioplasty.

In the Cardiac Catheterization Laboratory, he was found to
have a totally occluded LAD which was stented.  His RCA was
80% stenosed and no intervention was done.  The LAD had a
distal coronary dissection in the Catheterization Laboratory.

The patient was admitted to the CCU after catheterization.

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY:
1.  Liver transplant six years ago for hepatitis B and
hepatitis C.  The patient is followed at Continuecare Hospital At Medical Center Odessa.
2.  Heroin abuse, on methadone maintenance.
3.  Diabetes mellitus.
4.  Recent community-acquired pneumonia with recent discharge
from Desert View Regional Medical Center Hospital for this pneumonia.
5.  Chronic renal insufficiency with a baseline of 2.1.
6.  Hypertension.

ALLERGIES:  The patient is allergic to aspirin, codeine.

MEDICATIONS AT HOME:
1.  Prednisone 7 mg p.o. q.d.
2.  Prograf 3 mg p.o. b.i.d.
3.  Prozac.
4.  Methadone 95 mg p.o. q.d.
5.  NPH 20 units at night, 20 in the morning.
6.  Regular insulin sliding scale.
7.  Norvasc 5 mg p.o. q.d.

SOCIAL HISTORY:  The patient is a current smoker of one pack
per day times 40 years.  He does not currently drink alcohol
but has a history of alcohol abuse.  He denied current IV
drug use, although he was a past heroin user.

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION ON ADMISSION:  The patient was somnolent
on admission.  HEENT examination showed anicteric sclerae.
The extraocular movements were intact.  The neck was supple.
The lungs were clear to auscultation anterolaterally.
Cardiovascular:  No rubs, gallops, or murmurs, S1, S2.  The
abdomen revealed a well-healed scar from his liver
transplant.  His belly was soft, nontender, nondistended,
with normoactive bowel sounds.  The extremities showed no
edema. The catheterization site was clean, dry, and intact
with no bruit or hematoma. The pulses were 2+ dorsalis pedis
bilaterally.  Neurologically, he was arousable but somnolent.

RESULTS OF THE CARDIAC CATHETERIZATION:  Cardiac output 3.42,
cardiac index 1.7, right atrial pressure 5, right ventricle
37/2, wedge 18, PA pressure 31/16.  MCA was open.  LAD with
proximal total occlusion.  Circumflex with minimally
obstructive disease.  RCA 80% mid, no intervention at that
time.  A PTCA and stent of the LAD with TIMI-III flow was
achieved.

LABORATORY DATA ON ADMISSION:  The white blood count was
12,000, hematocrit 32, platelets 178,000.  PT 12.9, PTT 36.8,
INR 1.1.  Sodium 136, potassium 5.3, chloride 105,
bicarbonate 20, BUN 41, creatinine 2.2, glucose 90, MB
fraction 680 with CK of 2,751, troponin greater than 50, ALT
86, AST 366, LDH 871, alkaline phosphatase 77, total
bilirubin 0.8.  The patient's U/A showed moderate blood,
greater than 300 protein, 8 red cells, 3 white cells, 3
epithelials, moderate bacteria, and 500 glucose.

The EKG on admission showed sinus tachycardia at 110, normal
axis and intervals, Q waves in V2 and V3, and ST elevations
in V2 through V5, 2 mm in V3 and V4.

The chest x-ray on admission showed mild CHF with left
greater than right and normal mediastinum, question of
retrocardiac infiltrate.

HOSPITAL COURSE:  This is a 58-year-old male with a history
of liver transplant, hepatitis B, hepatitis C, heroin abuse
who presents after having an anterior ST elevation MI with Q
waves on presentation, now status post angioplasty and stent
of the LAD with current RCA 80% stenosis without
intervention.

1.  CARDIAC:  A.  CAD: This is a diabetic male with two
vessel coronary artery disease.  His RCA was not
revascularized during his cardiac catheterization.  He had a
LAD distal coronary dissection.  This was without tamponade
or perforation.  He will likely need a CABG for his
revascularization considering he is a diabetic male with two
vessel disease.  He has been continued on his Plavix,
dipyridamole for his aspirin allergy, beta blocker, and
statin.  His hematocrit goal is above 30 with his history of
CAD and anemia.

PUMP:  The patient's EF was 30% status post his anterior MI.
He was initially given one dose of Lasix on admission with no
urine output.  The patient later diuresed well to 120 of
Lasix and was maintained with even ins and outs since then
this admission.  He currently has no symptoms of heart
failure at this time.

EP:  The patient has no current EP issues.

BLOOD PRESSURE:  The patient's blood pressure has been stable
for this admission and has not required pressors.  His blood
pressure was at the low normal range of 100 systolic on
admission and there was a question whether he had adrenal
insufficiency with his history of being on prednisone.  A
Cosyntropin stimulation test was done and he was found to
have borderline adrenal insufficiency, however, no
stress-dose steroids were prescribed at this time because of
his large anterior MI and risk of perforation with steroids.

2.  PULMONARY/ID:  The patient initially had a large oxygen
requirement, requiring a nonrebreather mask on his initial
night of admission.  His oxygen requirement was
multifactorial with a component of his pneumonia, likely
COPD, and as later seen on his CT scan, severe atelectasis at
his bilateral bases.

His pulmonary status improved well after antibiotics,
nebulizers, and aggressive pulmonary toilet, incentive
spirometry, and getting out of bed.  The patient had a small
left pleural effusion that was small enough that it was not
tapped.  He is currently on room air and saturating at 95%.
He will continue his Levaquin for four more days to complete
a ten day course.

3.  RENAL:  The patient has a history of chronic renal
insufficiency with a creatinine bump from 2 to 3 status post
cardiac catheterization.  His creatinine is now stable at 3.
He likely had some contrast nephropathy this admission.
Continue to follow his renal function, although he is making
good urine at this time.

4.  GASTROINTESTINAL:  The patient is status post liver
transplant.  He is being followed by the Hepatology Service.
His FK trough was initially high and his dose decreased from
3 to 2 b.i.d.  A FK trough level on this dose is pending at
this time.  He has no other signs or symptoms referable to
his liver disease and his LFTs have been normalized at this
time.

5.  HEMATOLOGY:  The patient had anemia on admission.  He was
initially transfused 1 unit to bring his hematocrit above 30.
After this, he had a central line placed and overnight the
cap of one of the parts came off and he oozed and lost about
1 to 2 units of blood.  He was transfused for this as well
with an appropriate hematocrit bump.  He has had one stool
guaiaced and this has been Guaiac negative.  His hemolysis
laboratories are also negative.  We will transfuse him to
keep his hematocrit above 30 and continue to Guaiac his
stools.  Likely his anemia is of chronic disease and some
blood loss.

He is also on heparin.  This is for his anterior wall motion
abnormality and low EF.  He will continue this until he goes
to CT surgery.

6.  ENDOCRINE:  The patient had a borderline abnormal
Cosyntropin stim test but will not get stress dose steroids
unless hypotensive because of the risk of perforation of his
large anterior MI.

He is currently on NPH 15 at night and 20 in the a.m. and
insulin sliding scale for his history of diabetes.  He has
had volatile blood sugars ranging anywhere from 50-400 during
this admission and continued to adjust his insulin regimen
p.r.n.

7.  FLUIDS, ELECTROLYTES, AND NUTRITION:  Mr. Kent had
multiple episodes of hyperkalemia on first presentation with
his new increase in his creatinine and decreased urine
output.  These responded well to Kayexalate and he had no EKG
changes at this time.  The goal is to keep him even; intake
equals out-take for the remainder of this admission.  He is
currently on a renal/diabetic diet.

8.  TUBES/SLIDES/AND DRAINS:  Mr. Kent has poor IV access
and a right IJ was placed in order to gain good access.  This
was done on 1972-03-29.

9.  PROPHYLAXIS:  Maintaining on a bowel regimen with
Protonix and the patient is out of bed to chair.  A speech
and swallow was done to evaluate him for question of
aspiration as a contribution to his pneumonia and this was
found to be negative for aspiration.

MEDICATIONS AT THE TIME OF DICTATION:
1.  Heparin GTT at 1,250 units an hour.
2.  Albuterol, ipratropium nebulizers.
3.  Methadone 95 mg p.o. q.d.
4.  Prednisone 7 mg p.o. q.d.
5.  Tacrolimus 2 mg p.o. b.i.d.
6.  Dipyridamole 75 mg p.o. q.i.d.
7.  Atorvostatin 20 mg p.o. q.d.
8.  Metoprolol 25 mg p.o. b.i.d.
9.  Pantoprazole 40 mg p.o. q.d.
10. Levofloxacin 250 mg p.o. q.d, day number six of ten.
11. Plavix 75 mg p.o. q.d.
12. NPH 15 at night, 20 in the morning.
13. Insulin sliding scale.
14. Isordil 10 mg p.o. t.i.d.

The rest of the Union County General Hospital hospital course will be dictated at
a later date.




                        DR.Lowe,Michelle 12-ABZ

Dictated By:[**Dictator Info **]
MEDQUIST36
